text: The subject of this ESCP video is the MR imaging of cryptoglandular perianal fistulas. This video is aimed at surgical trainees and young surgeons in order to gain knowledge about patient selection, application and basic interpretation of MR images of perianal fistulas. There are many treatment modalities available for cryptoglandular fistulas. Gold standard is still simple fistulotomy. This procedure is almost always effective, but can result in serious continence impairment when performed in the wrong patient. In patients with fistulas that traverse the middle or upper part of the external sphincter, sphincter preserving procedures should at least be considered.

MRI can help in selection. Let me show you some comparative anatomy. Coronal comparative anatomy is relatively simple to comprehend for the surgical mind, because it is similar to how we have studied anatomy from our anatomy textbooks. It is relatively easy to recognize the external anal sphincter, the internal anal sphincter, and the levator ani muscle.

Transverse comparative anatomy is somewhat more difficult, for reason that the transverse view at the top of the anal canal is quite different from the view at the bottom. Cephalad of the anal canal, the external sphincter is continuous with the puborectalis muscle. Because of this, there seems to be an anterior sphincter defect. In fact, when we follow these ends it becomes clear that this is the puborectalis muscle. It is important to understand that this point marks the cephalad border of the anal sphincter. First and foremost we want to know whether or not there are any N-drained fluid collections. For this TII fat side images are most useful. A cursory glance will show you whether or not there are any collections of fluid or granulation tissue. This information is very important as excessive inflammation will decrease the chance of success of most procedures. In this image the perilevator fluid collection is marked in green. We chose to drain this collection by placing acetone. Upon exploration we found the fluid collection exactly where MRI predicted it to be.

The surgeon inserts a comfortable seat and identifies the internal opening of the fistula on the left anterior side using T-II images. The fistula, which encompasses almost the entire external anal sphincter, is addressed with a lift procedure. The intrasphincteric tract is located, sloughed, and ligated using transfixion sutures on both ends, followed by disconnecting the fistula. Adequate closure is tested by injecting saline into the external opening.

The TII images show the fistula clearly at different levels. The external and internal anal sphincters are identified and delineated, revealing that the fistula track does not traverse the external anal sphincter, classifying it as an intersphincteric fistula.

On TII images, the fistula is identified and delineated along with the external and internal anal sphincters. The fistula is observed traversing the external anal sphincter, indicating a transventricular fistula. The distinction between low and high transventricular fistulas is assessed, with low fistulas requiring minimal sphincter cutting and high fistulas requiring extensive sphincter cutting for fistulotomy. Serial transverse images are used for accurate assessment of the surrounding sphincter.

The TII images display the anal canal, with the top, middle, and lower parts identified. The external anal sphincter is visible, spanning approximately three centimeters between images ten and twenty. The fistula is observed traversing the external anal sphincter at image thirteen.

I suggest you use a standardized fistula report form, such as the St. Mark's one. Several modified versions are available. Consistently using this type of form is helpful in developing an image of the perianal fistulas. The use of this report form is relatively easy. First draw the external opening based on physical examination. In this case anteriorly to the left. Then study MRI as we discussed before. First identify the fistula. Then identify the external anal sphincter at different levels of the anal canal. Finally, identify the internal anal sphincter. The location of the internal opening is then depicted in all three pictures. Finally, the entire fistula track is drawn, including an estimation or a calculation of the actual height of penetration of the external anal sphincter. I hope you find this video useful in your daily practice. If you have any additional questions or comments, please don't hesitate to contact me. Thank you very much for your attention.
